prompt: Why is ∃x(Dog(x) → Barks(x)) the WRONG translation of 'Some dogs bark'?
options: It is true even in a world with no dogs at all
It uses ∃ where it should use ∀
It claims that every dog barks
hint: A conditional A → B is automatically true whenever A is false. What does that do to ∃x(Dog(x) → ...)?
explanation: A conditional is true whenever its antecedent is false, so ∃x(Dog(x) → Barks(x)) is satisfied by ANY non-dog — it's even true in a world with zero dogs. It never guarantees that an actual dog barks. 'Some dogs bark' needs conjunction: ∃x(Dog(x) ∧ Barks(x)). This is the mirror image of the 'all' mistake: ∃ goes with ∧, ∀ goes with →.
